questions.prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
Power After the Storm
After two strong storms left West Glen without electricity for several days, the city studied how public buildings could remain useful during outages. The main concern was not only lighting. Residents needed places to charge phones, keep medicine cold, use medical devices, and receive reliable information. The city decided to test a small solar microgrid at the Oak Street Community Center.
A microgrid is a local energy system that can operate with the larger electric grid or separate from it during an outage. At Oak Street, rooftop solar panels charge a battery system during the day. When the main grid is working, the center uses regular electricity and sends extra solar power back to the utility. When an outage occurs, the battery can power selected areas of the building, including a cooling room, phone-charging stations, emergency lights, and a small kitchen.
The project has limits. The battery cannot power the entire building for several days, and cloudy weather reduces how quickly it recharges. City staff had to decide which services mattered most during an emergency. They also trained volunteers to check equipment and guide visitors during outages.
During a winter outage the following year, the center stayed open for nine hours after nearby homes lost power. More than sixty residents used the charging stations, and several people stored refrigerated medicine in a monitored cooler. The test did not solve every problem caused by storms, but it showed how careful planning can make one public building a dependable neighborhood resource.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: A solar microgrid helped one community center provide limited but important services during power outages.
choices.B: West Glen should close all public buildings during storms.
choices.C: Solar panels can power every building without batteries or planning.
choices.D: The Oak Street Community Center stopped using electricity from the main grid.

questions.correctAnswerText: A solar microgrid helped one community center provide limited but important services during power outages.
questions.explanation: The passage explains how the microgrid powered selected services during outages while noting its limits.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Power After the Storm.”
Why did city staff have to choose which services the battery would support?

choices.A: The battery could not power the entire building for several days.
choices.B: Residents refused to use phone-charging stations.
choices.C: The solar panels worked only at night.
choices.D: The community center had no volunteers.

questions.correctAnswerText: The battery could not power the entire building for several days.
questions.explanation: Because battery capacity was limited, staff had to choose the most important emergency services.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Power After the Storm.”
Which TWO services could the microgrid support during an outage?

choices.A: Phone-charging stations
choices.B: Emergency lights
choices.C: Every home in West Glen
choices.D: A full city bus system
choices.E: All appliances in the entire building for a week

questions.correctAnswerText: Phone-charging stations | Emergency lights
questions.explanation: The passage specifically lists phone-charging stations and emergency lights as areas the battery could support.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Power After the Storm.”
What can be inferred from the winter outage described in the final paragraph?

choices.A: The microgrid provided practical help even though it had limited capacity.
choices.B: The microgrid failed as soon as nearby homes lost power.
choices.C: Residents had no need for charging stations.
choices.D: The city decided to remove the battery system.

questions.correctAnswerText: The microgrid provided practical help even though it had limited capacity.
questions.explanation: The center stayed open for nine hours and served residents, showing practical value despite limits.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Power After the Storm.”
In the sentence “The test did not solve every problem caused by storms, but it showed how careful planning can make one public building a dependable neighborhood resource,” the word dependable most nearly means [D1].
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=reliable
questions.explanation: In context, dependable means reliable or able to be counted on.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Power After the Storm.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Cloudy weather reduces how quickly the battery recharges.
tiles.B: More than sixty residents used the charging stations.
tiles.C: Volunteers were trained to check equipment and guide visitors.
tiles.D: The city removed the solar panels after the test.

targets.T2: Evidence that residents used the service
targets.T3: Evidence that planning involved people as well as technology

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=Cloudy weather reduces how quickly the battery recharges.; T2=More than sixty residents used the charging stations.; T3=Volunteers were trained to check equipment and guide visitors.
questions.explanation: Each tile matches the requested type of evidence.

questions.prompt:
Read the workplace document and answer the question.
Shift Swap Guidelines
To: HomeCare Plus Staff
From: Scheduling Department
Subject: Updated Shift Swap Procedure
Beginning June 1, all shift swaps must be submitted through the scheduling app at least 24 hours before the original shift begins. The change is intended to reduce missed visits and last-minute confusion.
Employees may request a swap only with another employee who is already approved for the same type of client visit. For example, a staff member assigned to a mobility-assistance visit may not swap with someone who has not completed mobility-assistance training. This rule protects clients and ensures that care plans are followed correctly.
Both employees must confirm the swap in the app. A swap is not final until the scheduling supervisor approves it. Employees should not assume that a text message between coworkers is enough to change the schedule.
Emergency situations will still be reviewed by phone. If you become ill or have a family emergency less than 24 hours before a shift, call the on-call supervisor immediately. Do not rely on the app alone for urgent changes.
Repeated failure to follow the procedure may result in a meeting with a supervisor. Questions about the app or eligibility for specific visit types may be directed to the scheduling department during office hours.
What is the main purpose of the document?

choices.A: To explain how employees should request and confirm shift swaps
choices.B: To announce that all client visits are canceled after June 1
choices.C: To encourage employees to arrange schedule changes only by text message
choices.D: To remove training requirements for mobility-assistance visits

questions.correctAnswerText: To explain how employees should request and confirm shift swaps
questions.explanation: The document describes the updated procedure for requesting, confirming, and approving shift swaps.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Shift Swap Guidelines.”
Why must employees swap only with someone approved for the same type of visit?

choices.A: To make the scheduling app easier to download
choices.B: To protect clients and ensure care plans are followed correctly
choices.C: To reduce the number of office hours
choices.D: To prevent employees from working more than one shift per week

questions.correctAnswerText: To protect clients and ensure care plans are followed correctly
questions.explanation: The document states that swaps must involve employees approved for the same visit type to protect clients and care plans.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Shift Swap Guidelines.”
Which TWO statements are supported by the document?

choices.A: Both employees must confirm the swap in the app.
choices.B: A text message between coworkers is enough to finalize a swap.
choices.C: Emergency situations less than 24 hours before a shift should be handled by calling the on-call supervisor.
choices.D: Employees may swap with any coworker, regardless of training.

questions.correctAnswerText: Both employees must confirm the swap in the app. | Emergency situations less than 24 hours before a shift should be handled by calling the on-call supervisor.
questions.explanation: Both statements are directly supported by the guidelines.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Shift Swap Guidelines.”
What must happen before a shift swap becomes final?

choices.A: The client must download the scheduling app.
choices.B: The scheduling supervisor must approve the swap.
choices.C: The employee must wait until after the missed visit.
choices.D: The employee must send a message to every coworker.

questions.correctAnswerText: The scheduling supervisor must approve the swap.
questions.explanation: The document states that a swap is not final until supervisor approval.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Shift Swap Guidelines.”
Which sentence gives the strongest reason for requiring supervisor approval?

choices.A: “Beginning June 1, all shift swaps must be submitted through the scheduling app.”
choices.B: “The change is intended to reduce missed visits and last-minute confusion.”
choices.C: “Questions about the app or eligibility for specific visit types may be directed to the scheduling department.”
choices.D: “Repeated failure to follow the procedure may result in a meeting with a supervisor.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “The change is intended to reduce missed visits and last-minute confusion.”
questions.explanation: This sentence explains the reason behind the updated approval process.

questions.prompt:
Read the argument and answer the question.
Keep Adult Art Classes Open
The city should continue funding evening art classes at the adult learning center. Some residents argue that the center should spend its limited budget only on job training and test preparation. Those services are important, but the argument assumes that art classes are merely recreational. In reality, adult art classes support several practical goals.
First, these classes bring people into the learning center who may later enroll in other programs. A person who feels nervous about returning to school may begin with a drawing or ceramics class, meet instructors, and become comfortable in the building. Last year, the center reported that 28 percent of students in evening art classes later attended at least one workshop on digital skills, English language learning, or career planning.
Second, art classes can improve community life. Students have painted murals for neighborhood events, designed posters for nonprofit groups, and donated handmade bowls to a food pantry fundraiser. These projects give learners a chance to practice planning, collaboration, and communication.
The city does not need to choose between practical education and creative education. A balanced adult learning center can help residents build job skills, prepare for exams, and participate more fully in community life. Cutting art classes would save money on paper, but it would also remove a welcoming doorway into lifelong learning.
What is the author’s main claim?

choices.A: Evening art classes should continue because they support learning, community participation, and practical skills.
choices.B: Adult learning centers should replace all job training with ceramics classes.
choices.C: City residents should pay private tutors instead of using public programs.
choices.D: Art classes should be offered only to children.

questions.correctAnswerText: Evening art classes should continue because they support learning, community participation, and practical skills.
questions.explanation: The author argues that art classes provide practical benefits beyond recreation.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Keep Adult Art Classes Open.”
Which evidence best supports the claim that art classes can lead students to other learning opportunities?

choices.A: Some residents prefer job training and test preparation.
choices.B: Students have donated handmade bowls to a fundraiser.
choices.C: Twenty-eight percent of evening art students later attended another workshop at the center.
choices.D: A balanced center can help residents prepare for exams.

questions.correctAnswerText: Twenty-eight percent of evening art students later attended another workshop at the center.
questions.explanation: This statistic supports the claim that art classes can lead students to other learning opportunities.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Keep Adult Art Classes Open.”
Which TWO practical benefits of art classes does the author describe?

choices.A: They may help hesitant adults become comfortable entering the learning center.
choices.B: They guarantee that every student will find a new job immediately.
choices.C: They can give students practice with planning, collaboration, and communication.
choices.D: They eliminate the need for test preparation.
choices.E: They require no funding from the city.

questions.correctAnswerText: They may help hesitant adults become comfortable entering the learning center. | They can give students practice with planning, collaboration, and communication.
questions.explanation: The passage describes both of these practical benefits.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Keep Adult Art Classes Open.”
How does the author respond to the opposing view that funding should go only to job training and test preparation?

choices.A: By saying that job training and test preparation are unimportant
choices.B: By arguing that art classes also support practical and educational goals
choices.C: By claiming that all adult students dislike career programs
choices.D: By suggesting that the learning center should close during the evening

questions.correctAnswerText: By arguing that art classes also support practical and educational goals
questions.explanation: The author responds by showing that art classes are not merely recreational.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Keep Adult Art Classes Open.”
Choose the sentence that would best strengthen the author’s argument if added after paragraph 3: [D1]

D1: Employers who attended a local hiring panel said teamwork and communication were among the skills they valued most.
The ceramics classroom has three sinks and two storage cabinets.
Many students prefer blue paint to green paint.

questions.correct: D1=Employers who attended a local hiring panel said teamwork and communication were among the skills they valued most.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=Employers who attended a local hiring panel said teamwork and communication were among the skills they valued most.
questions.explanation: This evidence strengthens the claim that skills practiced in art projects can have practical value.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Keep Adult Art Classes Open.”
Which assumption is most important to the author’s argument?

choices.A: Programs that seem creative can still produce practical benefits.
choices.B: Art classes are useful only when they cost more than other classes.
choices.C: Every adult learner wants to become a professional artist.
choices.D: Test preparation should be removed from the learning center.

questions.correctAnswerText: Programs that seem creative can still produce practical benefits.
questions.explanation: The argument depends on the idea that creative classes can support broader educational and workplace goals.

questions.prompt:
Read the narrative nonfiction excerpt and answer the question.
The Green Binder
When Nia became assistant manager at the grocery store, she inherited a green binder stuffed with handwritten notes. Some pages listed phone numbers for produce vendors. Others explained how to reset the checkout scale or where to find the extra receipt paper. The previous assistant manager had retired suddenly, and no one had organized the information.
At first, Nia kept the binder under the counter and searched through it whenever a problem appeared. On a busy Friday, the bakery printer jammed, a new cashier needed a register code, and a delivery driver asked for a form Nia had never seen. She flipped through loose pages while customers waited.
That evening, she stayed after closing. She sorted the binder into sections: vendors, equipment, forms, schedules, and emergency contacts. She threw away duplicate notes and typed instructions that were hard to read. On the inside cover, she added a page titled “Start Here When Something Breaks.”
A week later, the freezer alarm sounded during the lunch rush. Instead of calling three people in a panic, Nia opened the binder to the equipment section and followed the steps. Within minutes, she had moved frozen items to the backup case and called the repair number.
The binder still looked ordinary. Its plastic cover was scratched, and one ring did not close properly. But to Nia, it had changed from a pile of other people’s memories into a tool she could trust.
What is the main problem Nia faces in the excerpt?

choices.A: She must organize confusing workplace information so she can handle problems efficiently.
choices.B: She must convince customers to shop at a different grocery store.
choices.C: She wants to replace the freezer with a bakery printer.
choices.D: She refuses to use written instructions at work.

questions.correctAnswerText: She must organize confusing workplace information so she can handle problems efficiently.
questions.explanation: Nia’s main challenge is turning a disorganized binder into a useful workplace tool.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Green Binder.”
Why does the author include the busy Friday scene?

choices.A: To show why the disorganized binder creates difficulties for Nia
choices.B: To explain that customers prefer handwritten notes
choices.C: To show that the grocery store no longer needs vendors
choices.D: To prove that the bakery printer cannot be repaired

questions.correctAnswerText: To show why the disorganized binder creates difficulties for Nia
questions.explanation: The busy Friday scene demonstrates how hard it is to find information quickly when the binder is disorganized.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Green Binder.”
What does Nia’s “Start Here When Something Breaks” page show about her?

choices.A: She wants to make the binder easier to use during urgent situations.
choices.B: She plans to remove all emergency contacts from the binder.
choices.C: She is trying to hide information from other employees.
choices.D: She believes written directions are unnecessary.

questions.correctAnswerText: She wants to make the binder easier to use during urgent situations.
questions.explanation: The “Start Here” page is designed to guide users quickly when something goes wrong.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “The Green Binder.”
Which TWO details show that Nia improves the usefulness of the binder?

choices.A: She sorts the binder into sections.
choices.B: She keeps the binder under the counter.
choices.C: She types instructions that are hard to read.
choices.D: She notices that the plastic cover is scratched.
choices.E: She waits for customers while flipping through loose pages.

questions.correctAnswerText: She sorts the binder into sections. | She types instructions that are hard to read.
questions.explanation: Both actions make the binder clearer and more useful.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Green Binder.”
The phrase “a tool she could trust” suggests that Nia now sees the binder as

choices.A: a dependable source of information.
choices.B: a record that should never be opened.
choices.C: a decoration for the counter.
choices.D: a problem that cannot be solved.

questions.correctAnswerText: a dependable source of information.
questions.explanation: The phrase means Nia now trusts the binder to help her solve problems.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Green Binder.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Nia flips through loose pages while customers wait.
tiles.B: Nia sorts the binder into sections.
tiles.C: Nia follows the freezer alarm instructions during the lunch rush.
tiles.D: Nia throws away the entire binder.

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=Nia flips through loose pages while customers wait.; T2=Nia sorts the binder into sections.; T3=Nia follows the freezer alarm instructions during the lunch rush.
questions.explanation: The tiles show the problem, the solution, and evidence that the solution works.

questions.prompt:
Read the informational passage and answer the question.
Reading an Energy Label
When people buy a refrigerator, water heater, or air conditioner, they often focus on the purchase price. That price matters, but it is only part of the total cost. Appliances also use energy for years, and a less expensive model may cost more over time if it uses much more electricity.
Energy labels help shoppers compare models. A typical label estimates how much energy an appliance uses in one year and how that use compares with similar models. Some labels also include an estimated yearly operating cost based on average energy prices. The exact cost for a household may differ because utility rates and usage habits vary. Still, the label gives shoppers a common starting point.
The most useful comparison is usually between appliances of the same size and type. Comparing a large refrigerator with a small one may not be helpful because larger models naturally use more energy. Instead, a shopper should compare several refrigerators with similar storage capacity and features. If two models are nearly the same in price, the one with lower yearly energy use may be the better long-term value.
Energy labels do not tell shoppers everything. They do not measure noise, shelf layout, repair history, or whether an appliance fits the space available. However, they can prevent a common mistake: choosing the lowest price without considering years of operating costs.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Energy labels help shoppers consider long-term energy costs when comparing similar appliances.
choices.B: Shoppers should always buy the appliance with the highest purchase price.
choices.C: Energy labels show the exact monthly bill for every household.
choices.D: Appliance size has no effect on energy use.

questions.correctAnswerText: Energy labels help shoppers consider long-term energy costs when comparing similar appliances.
questions.explanation: The passage explains how energy labels help shoppers look beyond purchase price.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Reading an Energy Label.”
Why may the estimated yearly operating cost differ from a household’s actual cost?

choices.A: Utility rates and usage habits vary.
choices.B: Energy labels do not include any numbers.
choices.C: All appliances use the same amount of energy.
choices.D: Purchase price determines exact energy use.

questions.correctAnswerText: Utility rates and usage habits vary.
questions.explanation: Actual operating costs differ because electricity prices and household use are not the same for everyone.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Reading an Energy Label.”
Which comparison would be most useful according to the passage?

choices.A: A large refrigerator with a small desk lamp
choices.B: Several refrigerators with similar storage capacity and features
choices.C: A water heater with a laptop computer
choices.D: An old appliance manual with a store advertisement

questions.correctAnswerText: Several refrigerators with similar storage capacity and features
questions.explanation: The passage says the most useful comparison is usually between appliances of the same size and type.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Reading an Energy Label.”
Which detail best supports the idea that purchase price is not the only cost to consider?

choices.A: Appliances use energy for years after they are bought.
choices.B: Some labels are attached to refrigerators.
choices.C: Shelf layout may affect convenience.
choices.D: Shoppers often look at the purchase price.

questions.correctAnswerText: Appliances use energy for years after they are bought.
questions.explanation: This detail shows why long-term operating costs matter.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Reading an Energy Label.”
How is the passage mainly organized?

choices.A: It explains a problem, describes how labels help, and notes their limits.
choices.B: It tells a story about one family buying a refrigerator.
choices.C: It lists appliance brands from newest to oldest.
choices.D: It argues that shoppers should ignore operating costs.

questions.correctAnswerText: It explains a problem, describes how labels help, and notes their limits.
questions.explanation: The passage moves from purchase-price focus to the usefulness and limits of energy labels.

questions.prompt:
Read the editing passage and choose the best words for the blanks.
Library Seed Exchange
(1) The Arroyo Branch Library is starting a seed exchange for local gardeners. (2) Patrons may take up to five packets of vegetable or flower seeds each month. (3) Experienced gardeners are encouraged to donate extra seeds from plants that grew well in the area. (4) [D1], beginning gardeners can try new varieties without buying large packets. (5) Each packet [D2] labeled with the plant name and the year the seeds were collected. (6) The library will also offer short workshops on saving seeds safely and legally.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=As a result; D2=should be
questions.explanation: “As a result” shows the benefit of donated seeds, and “should be labeled” is grammatically correct.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Library Seed Exchange.”
Which revision of sentence 6 is most concise while keeping the meaning?

choices.A: The library will also offer short workshops on saving seeds safely and legally.
choices.B: The library will also offer workshops that are short in length and are about the subject of saving seeds in a safe and legal way.
choices.C: The library, which is a library, will also have workshops about seeds and saving.
choices.D: Short workshops will be offered by the library also on the topic of saving seeds.

questions.correctAnswerText: The library will also offer short workshops on saving seeds safely and legally.
questions.explanation: This version is concise and keeps the original meaning.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses correct subject-verb agreement?

choices.A: The packets of tomato seeds is stored in the front cabinet.
choices.B: The guide to local flowers are available at the desk.
choices.C: The volunteers who sort the donations work on Mondays.
choices.D: The box of labels have been placed near the printer.

questions.correctAnswerText: The volunteers who sort the donations work on Mondays.
questions.explanation: The plural subject “volunteers” correctly takes the plural verb “work.”

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the clearest pronoun reference?

choices.A: When Rosa showed the chart to Mei, she corrected it.
choices.B: Rosa showed the chart to Mei, and Mei corrected the chart.
choices.C: She corrected it when Rosa showed the chart to Mei.
choices.D: When it was shown to Mei by Rosa, she corrected it.

questions.correctAnswerText: Rosa showed the chart to Mei, and Mei corrected the chart.
questions.explanation: Repeating the nouns makes clear who corrected what.

questions.prompt:
Choose the best transition for the blank.
The repair crew expected the road work to finish by Friday. [D1], heavy rain delayed the final paving until Monday.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=However
questions.explanation: “However” correctly signals a contrast between the expected finish date and the delay.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is punctuated correctly?

choices.A: Because the hallway was crowded the staff opened another entrance.
choices.B: Because the hallway was crowded, the staff opened another entrance.
choices.C: Because, the hallway was crowded the staff opened another entrance.
choices.D: Because the hallway, was crowded the staff opened another entrance.

questions.correctAnswerText: Because the hallway was crowded, the staff opened another entrance.
questions.explanation: The introductory dependent clause should be followed by a comma.

questions.prompt:
Which revision best combines the sentences?
The clinic added Saturday hours. The hours are intended for working adults.

choices.A: The clinic added Saturday hours intended for working adults.
choices.B: The clinic added Saturday hours, they are intended for working adults.
choices.C: Intended for working adults, Saturday added the clinic hours.
choices.D: The hours added the clinic and were Saturday for adults.

questions.correctAnswerText: The clinic added Saturday hours intended for working adults.
questions.explanation: This revision combines the two ideas clearly and concisely.

questions.prompt: Which sentence maintains consistent verb tense?

choices.A: The technician inspected the machine and replaces the filter yesterday.
choices.B: The technician inspects the machine and replaced the filter yesterday.
choices.C: The technician inspected the machine and replaced the filter yesterday.
choices.D: The technician had inspected the machine and replaces the filter yesterday.

questions.correctAnswerText: The technician inspected the machine and replaced the filter yesterday.
questions.explanation: Both verbs are in past tense and match “yesterday.”

questions.prompt: Which sentence places the modifier correctly?

choices.A: Covered with handwritten notes, Jamal reviewed the bulletin board.
choices.B: Jamal reviewed the bulletin board covered with handwritten notes.
choices.C: The handwritten notes reviewed Jamal covered with the bulletin board.
choices.D: Covered with handwritten notes, the reviewing was done by Jamal.

questions.correctAnswerText: Jamal reviewed the bulletin board covered with handwritten notes.
questions.explanation: The modifier is placed next to “bulletin board,” the thing covered with notes.

questions.prompt:
Read the paired passages and answer the question.
Passage A: Turn the Vacant Lot into a Native Meadow
The vacant lot on Cedar Avenue should become a native meadow, not a fenced dog park. The lot is one of the few open spaces in the neighborhood large enough to support pollinator plants, shade trees, and a walking path. A meadow would give residents a quiet place to sit while also helping butterflies, bees, and birds that depend on local plants.
Maintenance would be manageable if the city planned carefully. Native plants often need extra watering and weeding during the first year, but once established, they usually require less mowing than grass. Community volunteers have already offered to help with planting days and seasonal cleanups. A meadow could also include signs explaining the plants and their importance, turning the space into a small outdoor classroom.
Dog owners deserve public space too, but the nearest dog park is already less than a mile away. Cedar Avenue has no garden or natural area within easy walking distance. A meadow would serve more residents and create environmental benefits that a dog park would not provide.
Passage B: Build the Cedar Avenue Dog Park
The Cedar Avenue lot should become a dog park because the neighborhood needs a safe, legal place for dogs to exercise off leash. Many apartment residents do not have yards, and the sidewalks around Cedar Avenue are crowded. A fenced dog park would give dogs room to run while reducing conflicts between dog owners, pedestrians, and drivers.
Supporters of a native meadow argue that the nearest dog park is less than a mile away. That distance may sound short, but it is difficult for residents without cars, older owners, or people walking in bad weather. A local dog park would also create a regular gathering place where neighbors could meet.
The city can design the park responsibly. Separate areas for large and small dogs, posted rules, waste stations, and shade trees would address many concerns. Native plants could be added around the fence and entrance. The choice is not between dogs and nature. With good design, a dog park can meet a real community need while still improving the appearance of the vacant lot.
What is the central difference between the two passages?

choices.A: Passage A favors a native meadow for environmental and quiet-use benefits, while Passage B favors a dog park for safe exercise and convenience.
choices.B: Passage A argues that Cedar Avenue should be paved for parking, while Passage B argues that the lot should remain locked.
choices.C: Passage A opposes all public spaces, while Passage B opposes all plants.
choices.D: Passage A focuses on library services, while Passage B focuses on grocery prices.

questions.correctAnswerText: Passage A favors a native meadow for environmental and quiet-use benefits, while Passage B favors a dog park for safe exercise and convenience.
questions.explanation: The passages disagree about the best use of the vacant lot.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about the Cedar Avenue lot.
Which evidence most directly supports Passage A’s argument?

choices.A: The lot is large enough to support pollinator plants, shade trees, and a walking path.
choices.B: Many apartment residents do not have yards.
choices.C: Separate areas can be built for large and small dogs.
choices.D: Sidewalks around Cedar Avenue are crowded.

questions.correctAnswerText: The lot is large enough to support pollinator plants, shade trees, and a walking path.
questions.explanation: This evidence directly supports Passage A’s argument for a native meadow.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to the paired passages about the Cedar Avenue lot.
Which TWO claims from Passage B are supported with reasons or evidence?

choices.A: A local dog park would help apartment residents whose dogs need off-leash exercise.
choices.B: A dog park would reduce every traffic problem in the city.
choices.C: Waste stations and posted rules could address some concerns about cleanliness and behavior.
choices.D: A native meadow would be illegal on Cedar Avenue.
choices.E: All residents prefer dog parks to quiet natural spaces.

questions.correctAnswerText: A local dog park would help apartment residents whose dogs need off-leash exercise. | Waste stations and posted rules could address some concerns about cleanliness and behavior.
questions.explanation: Passage B supports these claims with reasons and design features.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about the Cedar Avenue lot.
Which statement from Passage B directly responds to a point made in Passage A?

choices.A: “The Cedar Avenue lot should become a dog park.”
choices.B: “Supporters of a native meadow argue that the nearest dog park is less than a mile away.”
choices.C: “Many apartment residents do not have yards.”
choices.D: “The city can design the park responsibly.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Supporters of a native meadow argue that the nearest dog park is less than a mile away.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly responds to Passage A’s point about an existing dog park nearby.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about the Cedar Avenue lot.
Which statement is a supported inference from both passages?

choices.A: The vacant lot could be improved in more than one way.
choices.B: The city has already started building a dog park.
choices.C: No residents care about the future of the lot.
choices.D: The lot is too small for any public use.

questions.correctAnswerText: The vacant lot could be improved in more than one way.
questions.explanation: Both passages propose improvements, though they disagree about which one is better.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about the Cedar Avenue lot.
Which additional evidence would most help evaluate both arguments?

choices.A: A neighborhood survey showing how residents currently use open space and what needs they identify
choices.B: A list of dog names from apartment buildings
choices.C: A history of the company that once owned the vacant lot
choices.D: A map of restaurants outside the neighborhood

questions.correctAnswerText: A neighborhood survey showing how residents currently use open space and what needs they identify
questions.explanation: This evidence would help compare community demand for both proposed uses.

questions.prompt:
Which revision fixes the misplaced modifier?
Covered in dust, Tasha opened the storage box.

choices.A: Covered in dust, the storage box was opened by Tasha.
choices.B: Tasha opened the storage box covered in dust.
choices.C: The dust opened the storage box while Tasha watched.
choices.D: Opening the storage box, the dust covered Tasha.

questions.correctAnswerText: Tasha opened the storage box covered in dust.
questions.explanation: This revision places the modifier next to the storage box, which is the item covered in dust.

questions.prompt:
Which choice best combines the sentences without changing their meaning?
The center scheduled a workshop. The workshop explains tenant rights.

choices.A: The center scheduled a workshop explaining tenant rights.
choices.B: The workshop scheduled the center and explained tenant rights.
choices.C: The center scheduled, and tenant rights explained the workshop.
choices.D: Explaining tenant rights, the center was scheduled by the workshop.

questions.correctAnswerText: The center scheduled a workshop explaining tenant rights.
questions.explanation: This sentence clearly combines the two ideas without changing the meaning.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses commas correctly?

choices.A: The handbook, which was updated in April, explains the new procedure.
choices.B: The handbook which was updated, in April explains the new procedure.
choices.C: The handbook, which was updated in April explains, the new procedure.
choices.D: The handbook which, was updated in April, explains the new procedure.

questions.correctAnswerText: The handbook, which was updated in April, explains the new procedure.
questions.explanation: The nonessential clause “which was updated in April” is correctly set off by commas.

questions.prompt:
Read both passages. Write an evidence-based essay of 300–500 words explaining which position is better supported. Use evidence from both passages. Maintain a formal tone and organize your response clearly.
Passage A:
Use Remote Learning Days During Severe Weather
School districts should use remote learning days when severe weather makes travel unsafe. In the past, a snowstorm or flooding event often meant a full cancellation, followed by a make-up day added later in the year. Remote learning gives schools another option. Students can continue lessons from home while buses stay off dangerous roads.
Remote learning days do not need to copy a normal school day minute by minute. Teachers can post recorded explanations, reading assignments, practice problems, and short check-ins. Students who share devices or have limited internet access can be given printed packets at the start of the winter season, and assignments can remain open for several days. This flexible approach recognizes that weather emergencies affect families differently.
The benefit is continuity. When learning stops for several unexpected days, teachers must spend time reviewing material and adjusting schedules. Remote learning days can reduce that disruption. They can also help districts avoid extending the school year into summer, when families may have work, childcare, or travel plans. Severe weather days should still be used when power outages are widespread, but when conditions allow, remote learning is a practical solution.
Passage B:
Keep Severe Weather Days as True Days Off
School districts should not replace severe weather cancellations with remote learning days. When weather is dangerous enough to close school buildings, many families are also dealing with power outages, childcare problems, transportation issues, or the need to clear snow and ice. Expecting students to complete online lessons during those conditions may create stress without producing meaningful learning.
Remote learning also raises fairness concerns. Some students have quiet rooms, reliable internet, and adults available to help. Others share a phone with siblings, live in crowded housing, or lose internet service during storms. Printed packets may help, but they do not provide the same support as a teacher in the classroom. Students who already face challenges may fall further behind.
Traditional weather days also serve a safety purpose. They allow school staff, road crews, and families to focus on immediate needs rather than attendance logs and assignments. A few make-up days later in the year are a reasonable trade-off for protecting students’ well-being. Schools should reserve remote learning for planned situations, not emergencies that disrupt homes and communities.
Writing Task:
Which passage presents the better-supported argument about whether schools should use remote learning days during severe weather? Write an essay explaining your position. Use evidence from both passages to support your analysis.
Rubric Guidance:
• Analyze which argument is better supported.
• Use relevant evidence from both passages.
• Explain why the evidence supports your claim.
• Address weaknesses or limitations in the less-supported argument.
• Organize ideas logically.
• Use clear sentence structure, grammar, and formal language.
questions.correctAnswerText: Not auto-scored
questions.explanation: Extended Response answers should be reviewed using the rubric guidance. Strong responses make a clear claim, use evidence from both passages, explain why one argument is better supported, and maintain formal organization and language.
